title: LLM.int8(): 8-bit Matrix Multiplication for Transformers at Scale
abstract: LLM.int8() is a two-part quantization method for transformer-scale matrix multiplication. It combines vector-wise quantization (row/column scaling constants per inner product) with mixed-precision decomposition for sparse high-magnitude outlier feature dimensions. This addresses scale-dependent quantization failure in large LLMs and enables strong memory reduction while preserving model quality at high parameter counts.
notes_summary: Vector-wise quantization plus outlier-dimension decomposition is the core of LLM.int8() for large-scale LLM inference.
key_contributions: Analyzes emergent outlier features as the main source of large-scale quantization breakdown.
Introduces vector-wise quantization for higher inner-product precision.
Introduces mixed-precision decomposition: outliers in FP16, bulk in INT8.
Demonstrates quality-preserving scaling trend at large model sizes.
Provides practical open-source implementation path via bitsandbytes.
algorithms: Compute row/column scaling constants and quantize inputs/weights vector-wise.
Detect outlier feature dimensions above threshold alpha.
Compute outlier dimensions in FP16 and remaining dimensions in INT8.
Dequantize INT8 accumulation and add FP16 outlier contribution.
Apply across feed-forward and attention projection layers for full-model inference.

key_equations.name: Absmax quantization
key_equations.description: Tensor is scaled into Int8 range by infinity norm.

key_equations.name: Vector-wise matmul reconstruction
key_equations.description: Per-inner-product scaling improves precision over single tensor constant.

key_equations.name: Outlier set
key_equations.description: Dimensions with large magnitudes are isolated for high-precision path.

key_equations.name: Mixed-precision decomposition
key_equations.description: Combines sparse FP16 outlier computation with dense INT8 computation.

key_equations.name: Int matmul reconstruction factor
key_equations.description: Maps integer accumulation back to floating output domain.

architecture_detail:
## 1) Failure Mode at Scale

Large transformers develop sparse but systematic outlier feature dimensions. Single-scale quantization loses resolution for the bulk when these outliers dominate dynamic range.

## 2) Vector-wise Quantization

Instead of one tensor-level scaling constant, LLM.int8() uses row/column constants per inner product.

$$
\mathbf{C}_{f16}\approx\frac{1}{\mathbf{c}_{x}\otimes\mathbf{c}_{w}}\,\mathbf{C}_{i32}
$$

This improves quantization precision for most dimensions.

## 3) Mixed-Precision Decomposition

Outlier dimensions are separated and computed in FP16:

$$
O=\{i\mid |x^i|>\alpha\},\quad \alpha\approx6
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}_{f16}\approx\sum_{h\in O}\mathbf{X}_{f16}^h\mathbf{W}_{f16}^h + \mathbf{S}_{f16}\sum_{h\notin O}\mathbf{X}_{i8}^h\mathbf{W}_{i8}^h
$$

## 4) Why It Works

- Handles rare but critical outlier dimensions explicitly
- Preserves high throughput for dense non-outlier computation
- Improves scaling behavior of 8-bit inference in large models

## 5) Practical Notes

- Focus is on projection layers where parameter memory dominates
- Benefits are strongest at larger model dimensions
- Small models may not see net speedup after quant/dequant overhead

## 6) Implementation Checklist

- Tune and log outlier threshold alpha
- Separate kernel timings for quantization, GEMM, and dequantization
- Compare vector-wise only vs full decomposition
- Benchmark quality + memory + throughput together
- Lock framework/library versions for reproducibility
